highlights.title: Claude Code v2.1.197: Claude Sonnet 5 Is Now the Default Model
highlights.summary: Claude Sonnet 5 is now the default model in Claude Code, featuring a native 1M-token context window — enough to load entire large codebases in one session. It's available at promotional pricing of $2/$10 per million tokens through August 31, so update to v2.1.197 now to take advantage. This is a significant upgrade for anyone working on large projects where context limits were previously a bottleneck.

highlights.title: Claude Code v2.1.198: Background Agent Notifications, /dataviz Skill, and Chrome GA
highlights.summary: The latest release adds notification hooks for background agents — you'll now get alerted when a headless agent needs your input or finishes its task, making multi-agent workflows much easier to manage. A new `/dataviz` skill provides guided assistance for chart and dashboard design. Claude in Chrome is also now generally available, expanding where you can use Claude Code beyond the terminal.

highlights.title: Claude Code Is Steganographically Marking Its Requests
highlights.summary: A researcher discovered that Claude Code embeds hidden steganographic markers in the prompts it sends to the API, which can be used to identify requests as originating from Claude Code. This raises questions about transparency, privacy, and what those markers might be used for — especially relevant if you're building on top of Claude Code or routing its traffic. Worth reading if you care about what's happening under the hood of your AI coding tools.

highlights.title: Mastering Claude Code in 30 Minutes
highlights.summary: This YouTube tutorial promises a focused 30-minute walkthrough to get you productive with Claude Code, making it ideal if you want a structured overview without committing to a longer course. It's a great starting point for developers who've installed Claude Code but aren't sure how to integrate it into their daily workflow. Watch it alongside an open VS Code window to follow along in real time.

highlights.title: From Zero to Claude Code in 19 Minutes (No Code Required)
highlights.summary: This beginner-focused video walks you through setting up and using Claude Code from scratch without requiring any prior coding experience. At under 20 minutes, it's one of the quickest ways to go from never having used Claude Code to having it running on your machine. Perfect for non-developers or those new to AI-assisted coding who want a low-pressure introduction.

highlights.title: Claude Code v2.1.196: Org Default Models and Readable Session Names
highlights.summary: Organization admins can now set a default model for all members via the org console, which will appear as 'Org default' in the `/model` picker — great for teams that want to standardize on a specific model for cost or capability reasons. Sessions also now get readable default names at start, making it much easier to track and resume multiple parallel sessions. These are small but meaningful quality-of-life improvements for team-based Claude Code usage.

highlights.title: Best Claude Code Tutorial for Chinese Users: Step-by-Step Installation to Real Code Projects
highlights.summary: This Bilibili tutorial (originally titled: 'Absolutely the Best Claude Code Tutorial on Bilibili — Beginner-Friendly from Installation to Code Practice in China') offers a comprehensive guide specifically addressing the challenges of setting up and using Claude Code from mainland China. It covers installation, connecting from regions with restricted access, and real coding use cases. Supplementary materials are pinned in the video comments for download.

highlights.title: Shoaku: An IntelliJ Plugin to Help You Regain Coding Confidence After Using AI
highlights.summary: A developer noticed that relying on Claude Code and Codex sped up their work but eroded their own coding confidence — they were shipping AI-written code they didn't fully understand. Shoaku is an open-source IntelliJ plugin designed to act as a 'coding navigator,' helping you stay engaged and understand what AI-generated code is doing rather than blindly accepting it. A timely reminder that AI tools work best as a learning partner, not a replacement for understanding.

tips_of_the_day: With Claude Sonnet 5's 1M-token context window now active by default, try loading your entire project at once using `/add` or by pointing Claude at your repo root — you'll get much more coherent refactoring suggestions across files that previously had to be handled in chunks.

If you run multiple background agent sessions, give each one a descriptive task name at the start of your conversation. Now that sessions get readable default names and fire completion notifications, meaningful names make it far easier to know which agent finished and what to do next.

Before accepting a large block of AI-generated code, spend 60 seconds reading it out loud or explaining it to yourself. This simple habit keeps your understanding sharp and helps you catch subtle logic errors that look correct on first glance — the same problem the Shoaku plugin was built to solve.
